perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is subject to, and the Parties shall comply with, all valid present and future laws, regulations, rules and orders of Governmental Authorities now or hereafter having jurisdiction over the Parties, this Agreement, or the Services performed or the facilities utilized under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is subject to, and the Parties should endeavor to comply with, all present and future laws, regulations, rules and orders of Governmental Authorities now or hereafter having jurisdiction over the Parties, this Agreement, or the Services performed or the facilities utilized under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall comply' to 'should endeavor to comply' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, making compliance discretionary. This could lead to disputes about the level of effort required for compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: 16 TAC § 3.65 outlines specific requirements for oil and gas operators to comply with Railroad Commission rules. The original text ensures that the parties must comply with all rules. The modified text introduces ambiguity about the level of effort, contravening the mandatory compliance requirement mandated by Texas regulations. The Railroad Commission could find that 'endeavor to comply' is not sufficient to meet the standards for proper operation and safety.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Site Has Moved This Site Has Moved You will be redirected shortly to the new site. If not, click the button below: Go to New Site © 2025 Texas Secretary of State. All rights reserved.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall comply' to 'should endeavor to comply' weakens the legal obligation, introducing ambiguity about the level of effort required for compliance. This directly contradicts the intention of mandatory compliance as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, and Scraped Law Snippet 1 is irrelevant, the combination of the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and the clear weakening of mandatory language in the changed text establish a legal contradiction as it introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation of compliance, making enforcement difficult.

perturbation.original_text: Shipper shall release, indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Transporter and its affiliates, directors, officers, employees, agents, consultants, representatives, and invitees from and against all claims and losses arising out of or relating to (i) the operations of Shipper, (ii) any breach of this agreement by Shipper, except to the extent attributable to the negligence, willful misconduct or fault of Transporter, and (iii) operations or activities upstream or downstream of the Gathering System, except to the extent attributable to the negligence, willful misconduct or fault of Transporter.
perturbation.changed_text: Shipper will consider releasing, indemnifying, defending, and holding harmless Transporter and its affiliates, directors, officers, employees, agents, consultants, representatives, and invitees from and against all claims and losses arguably arising out of or relating to (i) the operations of Shipper, (ii) any potential breach of this agreement by Shipper, except to the extent attributable to the gross negligence, willful misconduct or fault of Transporter, and (iii) operations or activities upstream or downstream of the Gathering System, except to the extent arguably attributable to the gross negligence, willful misconduct or fault of Transporter.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'shall release, indemnify, defend, and hold harmless' to 'will consider releasing, indemnifying, defending, and holding harmless,' making the indemnification discretionary. Adding 'arguably' and changing 'negligence' to 'gross negligence' further weakens the obligation. This introduces ambiguity and contradicts the intent of indemnification clauses to provide clear protection against specific liabilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code § 127.005 addresses the enforceability of indemnity agreements related to motor carrier transportation. While this specific section deals with agreements concerning motor carriers, the general principle in Texas law is that indemnity agreements must be clear and unequivocal. The modified language uses 'will consider' and 'arguably,' which creates ambiguity and could make the indemnity clause unenforceable, thus conflicting with the law's requirement that indemnity agreements be clearly defined and agreed upon.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: the parties to joint operating agreements; (2) encourage mineral development; (3) are not against the public policy of this state; and (4) are enforceable unless those costs or losses are expressly excluded by written agreement. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Amended by Acts 1991, 72nd Leg., ch. 36, Sec. 2, eff. April 19, 1991. Sec. 127.003. AGREEMENT VOID AND UNENFORCEABLE. (a) Except as otherwise provided by this chapter, a covenant, promise, agreement, or understanding contained in, collateral to, or affecting an agreement pertaining to a well for oil, gas, or water or to a mine for a mineral is void if it purports to indemnify a person against loss or liability for damage that: (1) is caused by or results from the sole or concurrent negligence of the indemnitee, his agent or employee, or an individual contractor directly responsible to the indemnitee; and (2) arises from: (A) personal injury or death; (B) property injury; or (C) any other loss, damage, or expense that arises from personal injury, death, or property injury. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Sec. 127.004. EXCLUSIONS. This chapter does not apply to loss or liability for damages or an expense arising from: (1) personal injury, death, or property injury that results from radioactivity; (2) property injury that results from pollution, including cleanup and control of the pollutant; (3) property injury that results from reservoir or underground damage, including loss of oil, gas, other mineral substance, or water or the well bore itself; (4) personal injury, death, or property injury that results from the performance of services to control a wild well to protect the safety of the general public or to prevent depletion of vital natural resources; or (5) cost of control of a wild well, underground or above the surface. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Amended by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 1102, Sec. 2, eff. Sept. 1, 1989. Sec. 127.005. INSURANCE COVERAGE. (a) This chapter does not apply to an agreement that provides for indemnity if the parties agree in writing that the indemnity obligation will be supported by liability insurance coverage to be furnished by the indemnitor subject to the limitations specified in Subsection (b) or (c). (b) With respect to a mutual indemnity obligation, the indemnity obligation is limited to the extent of the coverage and dollar limits of insurance or qualified self-insurance each party as indemnitor has agreed to obtain for the benefit of the other party as indemnitee. (c) With respect to a unilateral indemnity obligation, the amount of insurance required may not exceed $500,000. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Amended by Acts 1989, 71st Leg., ch. 1102, Sec. 3, eff. Sept. 1, 1989; Acts 1991, 72nd Leg., ch. 36, Sec. 3, eff. April 19, 1991; Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 679, Sec. 1, eff. Aug. 28, 1995; Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 1006, Sec. 1, eff. Aug. 30, 1999. Sec. 127.006. INSURANCE CONTRACT; WORKERS' COMPENSATION. This chapter does not affect: (1) the validity of an insurance contract; or (2) a benefit conferred by the workers' compensation statutes of this state. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Sec. 127.007. OWNER OF SURFACE ESTATE. This chapter does not deprive an owner of the surface estate of the right to secure indemnity from a lessee, an operator, a contractor, or other person conducting operations for the exploration or production of minerals of the owner's land. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity by changing 'shall release, indemnify, defend, and hold harmless' to 'will consider releasing, indemnifying, defending, and holding harmless' and adding 'arguably.' This weakens the indemnification obligation, making it discretionary and unclear. According to the Law Explanation, Texas law requires indemnity agreements to be clear and unequivocal. The altered language fails to meet this requirement. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 primarily addresses indemnity agreements related to oil, gas, and water wells, it reflects a broader principle in Texas law that indemnity obligations must be clearly defined. The changes create an ambiguous obligation, which is a contradiction as the original text ensured a definite obligation.
